one_line_takeaway: Adding a momentum term to GCG's gradient heuristic (MAC) yields a substantial ASR and stability boost for universal multi-prompt jailbreak suffixes and better robustness to defenses, but only marginal gains for single-prompt attacks, in a small two-model, low-step, keyword-evaluated study.
key_finding: Momentum helps most in the harder universal (multiple-prompt) setting and is roughly neutral in the individual-prompt setting. On vicuna multiple-prompt attacks at mu=0.6, MAC reaches 48.6% average ASR vs 38.1% for GCG (+10.5 pts) and 86.1% vs 72.7% maximum ASR (+13.4 pts), while also cutting the standard deviation of maximum ASR from 15.36 to ~9. Individual-prompt gains are marginal (vicuna ~+1.3 pts average ASR, steps ~12.62 -> ~12.46). MAC also modestly improves transfer (e.g. suffix transferred to Mistral: 51.3% vs GCG 46.0%, vs 42.5% no-attack) and is markedly more robust to defenses: under the PPL filter MAC holds 76.0% ASR vs GCG's 28.0%. mu=0.6 is the reported sweet spot; too much momentum (mu=0.8) degrades performance.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Momentum Accelerated GCG (MAC), a white-box adversarial-suffix jailbreak attack that augments the Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) attack by adding a momentum term to the token-selection gradient heuristic. The authors reinterpret GCG's per-iteration suffix update as a step of stochastic gradient descent over the heuristic loss, and, by analogy to momentum SGD and to momentum-boosted adversarial attacks in vision (Dong et al. 2018), maintain a running momentum gradient g <- mu*g + (1-mu)*g_t that is then fed into GCG's top-k / random-substitution search. Two variants are given: MAC on an individual prompt (momentum accumulated across optimization steps, Algorithm 2) and MAC on multiple prompts for a universal suffix (momentum accumulated across prompts, Algorithm 3). Note mu=0 recovers vanilla GCG exactly, so MAC is a single-hyperparameter generalization of GCG rather than a new attack family. This is explicitly framed as a short, preliminary study.
threat_model: White-box jailbreak: the adversary has full access to the target aligned LLM's weights and gradients in order to optimize an adversarial suffix appended to a harmful request, driving the model to emit a compliant prefix (e.g. 'Sure, here's'). The paper also evaluates a transfer/black-box setting where a suffix optimized on a local surrogate is applied to another model, and a defended setting where the target is protected by input/inference-time defenses (perplexity filter, self-reminder, in-context defense). The stated attacker goal is efficient jailbreaking; the authors also motivate the work as a tool for developers to do faster red-teaming and robustness evaluation.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: multiple-prompt MAC only handles batch size 1; only momentum is explored (not Adam or other optimizers); only two 7B models are tested. Reviewer-added: (1) individual-prompt improvements are within or near the reported standard deviations, so much of the headline benefit is confined to the universal-suffix setting; (2) the multiple-prompt vicuna result at mu=0.6 has a very high average-ASR std (14.97), undercutting the stability narrative for that specific cell even though max-ASR std improves; (3) success is measured only by refusal-keyword matching, which the authors themselves note is unreliable, with no human or judge-model validation of actual harmfulness; (4) T is capped at 20 epochs (far below GCG's usual ~500), so results characterize a low-budget regime rather than converged attacks; (5) the AutoDAN attacks cited in related work are not used as experimental baselines - the only compared baseline is GCG (mu=0).

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al.) - 100 randomly selected harmful prompts
Target models: vicuna-7b and Mistral-7b (evaluated as attack targets, not training data)
quantitative_results: Individual prompt, vicuna: GCG (mu=0) avg ASR 75.0% / 12.62 steps; MAC mu=0.6 avg ASR 76.0% / 12.55 steps; MAC mu=0.4 76.2% / 12.46 steps (best-step cell); mu=0.8 drops to 72.4%
Individual prompt, Mistral: GCG (mu=0) avg ASR 83.8% / 5.88 steps; MAC mu=0.6 87.2% / 5.77 steps (best)
Multiple prompt, vicuna: GCG (mu=0) avg ASR 38.1% (std 8.66), max ASR 72.7% (std 15.36); MAC mu=0.6 avg ASR 48.6% (std 14.97), max ASR 86.1% (std 9.00)
Multiple prompt, Mistral: GCG (mu=0) avg ASR 58.72%, max ASR 80.3%; MAC mu=0.8 avg 60.5% / max 85.5%, MAC mu=0.6 avg 61.9% / max 82.5%
Transfer (Table IV): No-attack ASR vicuna 3.0% / Mistral 42.5%; GCG vicuna 11.3% / Mistral 46.0%; MAC mu=0.6 vicuna 12.3% / Mistral 51.3%
Under defense on vicuna (Table V), GCG vs MAC mu=0.5: No Defense 72.7% vs 86.1%; PPL filter 28.0% vs 76.0%; Self-reminder 65.8% vs 75.0%; ICD 1-shot 35.8% vs 62.5%
baselines_compared: GCG / Greedy Coordinate Gradient (Zou et al. 2023), instantiated as MAC with mu=0 - the sole experimental baseline
Defenses evaluated (not attack baselines): PPL filter (Jain et al.), Self-reminder (Xie et al.), ICD 1-shot (Wei et al.)
